---
title: "About"
---

# Research Focus

I am a National Science Foundation-Ocean Sciences Division Postdoctoral Research Fellow at the University of Nebraska-Lincoln, specializing in marine diatom biostratigraphy and paleoceanography. My research combines micropaleontological analysis with field studies to understand Earth's past climate and oceanographic changes.

# Current Projects

-   Investigating marine diatom and silicoflagellate responses to the Cretaceous-Paleogene extinction event

-   Studying early diatom evolution and seasonal deposition patterns in laminated sediments

-   Developing Antarctic Bottom Water circulation proxies using Southern Ocean marine diatoms

-   Contributing to the SWAIS2C project examining West Antarctic Ice Sheet stability

# Field Experience

My research has taken me from Antarctic ice streams to ocean drilling expeditions. Recent field work includes:

-   SWAIS2C (2023-2024): Diatom specialist and core handler for the West Antarctic Ice Sheet study

-   IODP Expedition 392 (2022): Shipboard scientist studying Cretaceous climates

-   IODP Expedition 361 (2016): Investigating the Agulhas and Benguela Currents

-   Whillans Ice Stream Subglacial Access Research Drilling (WISSARD) project: Diatom specialist and core handler for this project.

# Teaching and Mentorship

I am committed to education and mentorship in the geosciences, having taught courses in oceanography, physical geology, environmental geology, and earth history. I have mentored multiple undergraduate researchers in micropaleontological techniques and field studies. Student researchers have presented their results at conferences such as GSA.

# Professional Impact

My research has contributed to our understanding of:

-   Antarctic ice sheet dynamics and climate history

-   Marine diatom evolution and biostratigraphy

-   Ocean circulation patterns and their response to climate change

-   Cretaceous-Paleogene extinction event impacts on marine ecosystems

-   Developed an offshore reference section to build towards a more robust framework for correlating and dating the significant but fragmentary vertebrate-bearing sequences of the coastal Eastern Pisco Basin Stratigraphic sequences.

::: {.callout-note}
I am always interested in collaborating on projects involving marine diatoms, paleoceanography, and subglacial access studies. My work combines traditional microscopy with modern analytical techniques to unlock the secrets preserved in Earth's geological record.
:::
